highlights:
# Adaptive ML and AT&T Expand AI Collaboration to Scale Specialized Models Across Enterprise Workflows

Author: Adaptive ML
Published: 2026-06-10T08:30:00-04:00
Source: prnewswire.com (prnewswire.com)
Language: en

## Story

Adaptive ML and AT&T Expand AI Collaboration to Scale Specialized Models Across Enterprise Workflows Accessibility Statement Skip Navigation

NEW YORK, June 10, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Adaptive ML, the leader in Reinforcement Learning Operations (RLOps), today announced the renewal and expansion of its work with AT&T. Following a year of successful production deployment, AT&T has now doubled its software footprint within the Adaptive Engine platform and embedded Adaptive Forward Deployed Engineers (FDEs) to accelerate the transition from prototypes built using closed proprietary LLMs prototypes to autonomous, agentic systems.

By leveraging Adaptive's RLOps platform, AT&T has moved beyond prompt engineering to develop in-house reasoning models based on open-source that bridge the "last mile" of performance, achieving frontier-level accuracy while drastically reducing operational costs.

The contract renewal doubles AT&T's software capacity in terms of GPU nodes, allowing for more high-volume use cases. As part of the expansion, embedded FDEs will work directly within AT&T's data science teams to support the upskilling of internal teams and co-develop complex reasoning models for workflows like fraud detection and bilingual customer engagement.

The work with AT&T to date has already delivered transformative results:

- Call Summarization: Adaptive fine-tuned a Gemma 12B model that delivers 30% faster performance over general-purpose APIs in providing daily transcripts for customer service calls.
- Fraud Detection: Specialized reasoning models reduce fraud case review time from six minutes to just 30 seconds, up to 12x more throughput for fraud analysts.

"At AT&T, our goal has always been to prove AI's value in enterprise-grade operations, instead of deploying demos as science projects," said Mark Austin, Vice President of Data Science at AT&T. "To get the most value out of agentic AI applications, we need to move beyond generic prompts and specialize our models on our own data and workflows. Our work with Adaptive ML provides the critical compute and data foundations required to make that jump and deliver real value for our employees and a better experience for our customers."

"AT&T is proving that the future of enterprise AI isn't just about bigger models, but is about owning the intelligence layer," added Julien Launay, CEO and Co-founder of Adaptive ML. "By focusing on specialization, AT&T is ensuring their domain-specific data becomes a compounding strategic asset. We are proud to provide the RLOps infrastructure for one of the most sophisticated AI deployments in the world."

About Adaptive ML Adaptive ML is a frontier AI startup developing the world's first Reinforcement Learning Operations (RLOps) platform for enterprise specialization. The Adaptive Engine is the indispensable intelligence and value layer enterprises need to bridge their compute and value layers. Headquartered in New York with a research team in Paris, Adaptive focuses on large-scale deployments within complex global organizations.

As described herein, various embodiments employ artificial intelligence (AI) to facilitate automating one or more features described herein. The embodiments (e.g., in connection with automatically facilitating network resiliency planning and/or remediation utilizing generative artificial intelligence (AI)) can employ various AI-based schemes for carrying out various embodiments thereof. Moreover, a classifier can be employed to determine a ranking or priority of each access point, base station, and/or antenna. A classifier is a function that maps an input attribute vector, x=(x1, x2, x3, x4 . . . xn), to a confidence that the input belongs to a class, that is, f(x)=confidence (class). Such classification can employ a probabilistic and/or statistical-based analysis (e.g., factoring into the analysis utilities and costs) to determine or infer an action that a user desires to be automatically performed. A support vector machine (SVM) is an example of a classifier that can be employed. The SVM operates by finding a hypersurface in the space of possible inputs, which the hypersurface attempts to split the triggering criteria from the non-triggering events. Intuitively, this makes the classification correct for testing data that is near, but not identical to training data. Other directed and undirected model classification approaches comprise, e.g., naïve Bayes, Bayesian networks, decision trees, neural networks, fuzzy logic models, and probabilistic classification models providing different patterns of independence can be employed. Classification as used herein also is inclusive of statistical regression that is utilized to develop models of priority.
[...]
As will be readily appreciated, one or more of the embodiments can employ classifiers that are explicitly trained (e.g., via a generic training data) as well as implicitly trained (e.g., via observing UE behavior, operator preferences, historical information, receiving extrinsic information). For example, SVMs can be configured via a learning or training phase within a classifier constructor and feature selection module. Thus, the classifier(s) can be used to automatically learn and perform a number of functions, including but not limited to determining according to predetermined criteria which of the access point(s), base station(s), and/or antenna(s) is to receive priority.

results.title: How My HPC Research Landed Me an Interview at AT&T Labs’ 5G/AV Division | by Jeel Patel | May, 2026 | Medium
highlights:
I did not apply to AT&T Labs. Not once. Not through the portal. Not through a referral. Not through a job board. A recruiter found my LinkedIn on a regular Thursday and sent me a message that would lead to one of the most interesting technical interviews of my graduate career.
[...]
The role was Research Software Engineer at AT&T Labs. The team was building infrastructure for next generation use cases in the 5G space — autonomous vehicles and drone navigation specifically. On paper I looked like someone who had never touched that domain. My profile talked about GPU clusters and synthetic workload generation and HPC job scheduling. Telecom was nowhere in my vocabulary.
[...]
At the NYU HPC Research Lab I was the Graduate Research Lead working on a problem that most people outside HPC have never thought about: how do you generate realistic synthetic GPU workloads for supercomputing clusters when you only have access to historical telemetry?
[...]
The architecture I built was a two stage generative system. The first stage was a Conditional Variational Autoencoder with a 32 dimensional latent space that encoded static job metadata — things like job class and resource requests and wall time. The second stage was a GRU decoder with 256 hidden units that generated variable length GPU telemetry sequences at 100ms resolution. The whole thing was trained on roughly 100 thousand job files from the MIT Supercloud dataset running on an NVIDIA L40S.
[...]
That is not a 5G project. That is not an autonomous vehicles project
[...]
defend in front of peers
[...]
A recruiter sent a short note about an AT&T Labs research role that needed someone with GPU experience and systems depth. He mentioned the team was doing work on spatial computing problems adjacent to autonomous systems. He asked if I would be open to a conversation.
[...]
I said yes. We talked. He explained the team and the project. I explained my research. By the end of that call he said my background was a strong fit and asked if I could do a technical screen the following week.
[...]
That phone screen was with a senior engineer on the team. It was a project conversation followed by a LeetCode medium — I walked through my architecture decisions and the reasoning behind key design choices, then moved to code. I cleared it.
[...]
Two weeks later I was invited to a 4 hour onsite.
[...]
This is the round nobody prepares for. No LeetCode platform. No autocomplete. Just a blank editor and a prompt to implement a data structure from scratch in C++. For a team building spatial infrastructure for drones and autonomous vehicles the natural candidate is a quad tree or a k-d tree — the canonical spatial indexes for this domain.
[...]
My NYU HPC Lab work was the answer to almost every question here. Leading a research project where the data pipeline was broken for two weeks. Defending architectural choices to a faculty advisor who disagreed. Shipping something that was 80% right because the alternative was shipping nothing.
[...]
Research transfers across domains in ways that job titles never do. I had no 5G experience. I had no AV experience. But I had built something serious on GPUs and I had made real engineering decisions and I had results I could defend. That was enough to get me in the room.
[...]
Your LinkedIn headline is doing more work than you think. The recruiter did not find me because I listed Python and C++. He found me because my headline and about section described a concrete problem I had solved. “Graduate Research Lead building generative architectures for HPC workload synthesis” is infinitely more findable than “MS Computer Engineering student seeking full time roles.”
[...]
It will translate. The domain does not matter as much as the depth. A recruiter looking for someone who understands GPU memory constraints and spatial data structures and systems performance is going to find your HPC work interesting even if you have never written a single line of 5G code.

results.title: METHOD AND APPARATUS FOR EFFICIENTLY PERFORMING DISCRETE MONTE CARLO SIMULATION ANALYSIS IN NETWORK-BASED MICROSERVICES - Patent Application
highlights:
Smart Summary: An efficient way to run simulations, like Monte Carlo simulations, on dynamic systems is introduced. An AI model is trained to adjust data samples related to these systems, helping to make the data smaller and more manageable. When a client requests a simulation, the system checks network conditions, like speed and delays, to set the right parameters for the analysis. It then retrieves a smaller version of the data samples from a database and rebuilds them into a complete set using mathematical methods. Finally, the results of the simulation are sent back to the client quickly, improving response times and reducing the load on the network while keeping the quality of the simulation high. 🚀 TL;DR
[...]
Aspects of the subject disclosure may include, for example, an efficient approach for performing simulation analyses, such as Monte Carlo simulations, on dynamic systems using a network-based processing system. The method may involve training an artificial intelligence (AI) model to modify data samples associated with a dynamic system. The AI model can utilize classification techniques to determine how to reduce and optimize the data samples. Upon receiving a request from a client device, the processing system can analyze network conditions, including bandwidth and latency, to determine operational parameters for the simulation. A modified set of data samples, reduced based on the operational parameters, can be retrieved from a database server. The processing system can restore the reduced set to a full data set through mathematical transformations, including interpolation, and uses the reconstructed data for the simulation analysis. The simulation result can be transmitted back to the client device, thereby enabling faster response times and reduced network demands while maintaining simulation quality.

The communication device 600 can serve as an illustrative embodiment of devices such as data terminals 114, mobile devices 124, vehicle 126, display devices 144 or other client devices for communication via either communications network 125. For example, computing device 600 can facilitate, in whole or in part, providing a Monte Carlo simulation via a microservice. A request for a Monte Carlo simulation of a dynamic system can be received. A request for quantile samples for variables of the dynamic system can be sent to database server. A reduce-size set of quantile samples can be received from the database server over a network. A standard set of M quantile samples, which divides the variables into 1/M probability steps, can be converted to a reduced sized set of N quantile samples, which divide the variables into 1/N probability steps. By sending a reduced size set of quantile samples from the database server the network demands are decreased, and the speed is increased. Interpolation can be performed on the reduced size set of quantile samples to generate a restored size set of quantile samples. The Monte Carlo simulation can be performed on the restored size set of quantile samples and the results can be reported to a requesting client device.
